topic: Patterns - Phantom Data for API Versioning
content: When designing a versioned API in Rust, the primary goal is to guarantee compile-time safety against breaking changes while maintaining runtime efficiency. The canonical solution involves using phantom type parameters within generic structs or enums, specifically leveraging `std::marker::PhantomData` to encode version information without consuming heap memory. This technique allows you to separate the data payload from the version contract, ensuring that V1 and V2 variants can coexist in a single enum definition without polluting the address space with actual pointers or instances of the data types themselves. Consider an API trait where different versions expose different methods; by attaching a PhantomData<V> parameter to each variant, you prevent users from accidentally calling a V2 method on a V1 object because the compiler will reject such calls at compile time due to type mismatches. This approach is strictly superior to runtime version checking or string-based tagging because it eliminates the possibility of silent data corruption caused by deserializing an old format into a new structure without explicit validation. From a memory perspective, phantom types are zero-cost abstractions; they do not allocate any stack or heap space since the compiler discards them entirely during code generation. You can safely store millions of versions in your API registry without worrying about memory bloat, as long as you avoid holding actual instances of the data structures inside the enum variants themselves. The mental model to adopt is that phantom parameters act as logical bookmarks rather than physical storage; they tell the compiler "this variant represents version N" but do not carry any bytes into the binary image. When implementing this pattern in a library like serde or derive macros, ensure you explicitly name your PhantomData instances with clear identifiers such as `PhantomData<V1>` to make debugging version mismatches straightforward. You must never confuse phantom parameters with actual data fields; if you accidentally store a real instance of T inside the enum variant alongside the phantom marker, you will introduce unnecessary heap allocations and potential memory leaks when cloning or copying versions. The performance implication is that your API remains as fast as a flat struct for iteration over payloads, since no additional indirection exists beyond what the compiler already handles for generics. Finally, remember that while this pattern provides strong guarantees, it does not replace semantic versioning policies; you must still document breaking changes in your changelog because compile-time type safety only catches syntactic errors between versions, not logical inconsistencies in the API contract itself.

topic: Patterns - Phantom Data for API Versioning
content: When I first started designing a public API in Rust, the most painful lesson came from trying to version my endpoints without using phantom data. Many languages force you to include actual fields like an `id` or a `timestamp` inside every request object just to distinguish versions, which bloats your payload and breaks down when those fields change semantically between V1 and V2. In Rust, we solve this elegantly by leveraging the phantom type pattern through generics where the generic parameter carries no runtime data but acts as a compile-time marker for API versioning. Let me explain why this is so powerful: imagine you have an `ApiRequest` struct that needs to support both Version 1 and Version 2 of your endpoint, where V1 uses a simple string field while V2 replaces it with a structured object containing nested types like `Vec<String>` or specific enums. If you just create two separate structs named `V1Request` and `V2Request`, you lose the ability to share common logic between them, such as validation traits or serialization behavior, because each struct is isolated in its own type definition. By introducing a generic parameter with a phantom data field like `PhantomData<V>` inside your base request struct, you can keep a single `ApiRequest<T>` that adapts based on the version, where `T` represents the payload shape and `V` represents the API version itself. The compiler enforces this strictly: if you try to serialize an `ApiRequest<PayloadType1>` but your code expects it to be compatible with V2 serialization logic, the build fails immediately because the phantom tag prevents accidental mixing of incompatible versions at runtime. This approach aligns perfectly with Rust’s ownership and borrowing rules since there is no hidden state being mutated or copied; the version information lives entirely in the type system. I remember a senior engineer once told me that this pattern feels like magic to developers coming from dynamic languages because they can’t easily inspect what `T` is at runtime without breaking the contract, yet it guarantees safety where static analysis alone cannot catch all mistakes. When implementing this with crates like `serde` or `axum`, you simply define your base struct with a generic parameter constrained by a trait bound that ensures version-specific logic only applies to the correct phantom variant, making your API surface both flexible and immutable regarding breaking changes. The beauty is that when you need to add V3 later, you don’t have to refactor every existing handler; you just introduce `V3` as another valid instance of the same generic type, and the compiler will refuse to let you accidentally use a request meant for V2 in a context expecting V3 unless you explicitly match on the version enum. This level of compile-time enforcement means your API stays safe against subtle bugs that often slip through in other languages where runtime checks are too late or expensive. So the next time you’re designing an API, don’t just document versions in separate files; use phantom data to embed the version directly into the type system itself, ensuring every request is validated by the compiler before it ever touches your application logic.
